text: The surgeon begins dissection on the right lateral peritoneum at the level of the sacral promontory, using robotic assistance to carefully separate the tissue.
The surgeon opens the peritoneum along the length of the pelvis using robotic assistance, revealing underlying structures within the pelvic cavity.
The surgeon continues the dissection into the rectovaginal septum, using robotic instruments to carefully separate the tissue layers in this area.
A vaginal retractor is inserted into the vagina to enhance exposure of the surgical field.

The surgeon employs both sharp and blunt dissection techniques to carefully separate tissue layers within the pelvic region, continuing the dissection initiated on the right lateral peritoneum at the level of the sacral promontory.
The surgeon exposes the levator ani muscles at the upper part of the anal canal after completing dissection around the distal part of the rectum on both sides.
The surgeon estimates the distance to be two to three centimeters above the dentate line, confirmed visually during a digital rectal examination.
The surgeon continues dissection of the rectovaginal septum, employing traction and countertraction techniques to effectively separate the tissue layers.
The surgeon leaves fatty tissue on the posterior surface of the vagina, creating an additional protective layer to prevent mesh erosion during the rectopexy procedure.
The surgeon avoids dissecting the lateral stalks of the rectum and the posterior surface of the mesorectum, maintaining the integrity of these structures during the procedure.
The rectum is positioned within the presacral space, indicating its placement after dissection.
The surgeon applies and spreads the non-resorbable L-shaped Marcelin mesh anteriorly over the rectum, with the long limb positioned on the right side.
The surgeon positions the mesh as low as possible between the rectum and vagina, ensuring it is interposed to support the anatomical structures during the rectopexy procedure.
The surgeon carefully sutures the lower end of the mesh to the levator muscles on either side of the rectum, securing the mesh in place for the rectopexy procedure.
The surgeon positions the non-resorbable L-shaped Marcelin mesh adjacent to the rectum, ensuring it does not directly contact the rectal tissue to prevent mesh erosion.
The surgeon dissects an avascular area over the promontory and anterior prevertebral ligament, preparing the site for mesh attachment.
The surgeon fixes the mesh to the sacral promontory using one or two stitches, ensuring there is no tension on the mesh.
The surgeon places an additional suture to secure the upper part of the vagina to the mesh, effectively obliterating the Douglas pouch.
The surgeon closes the peritoneum over the mesh, covering the previously secured structures and completing the rectopexy procedure.
